The prophet describes the most depressing part of his experience and how he found support and relief. During his trial, the Lord seemed terrible to him. It was an affliction that felt like complete misery; for sin makes the cup of suffering a bitter one. The struggle between doubt and faith is often very difficult. But even the weakest believer is mistaken if they think their strength and hope have vanished from the Lord.

After describing his distress and temptation, the prophet shows how he was lifted above it. Even though things are bad, it is due to God’s mercy that they are not worse. We should notice what benefits us as well as what works against us. God's compassion never fails; we have fresh examples of this every morning. Possessions on earth are temporary, but God is a portion forever. It is our duty, and will be our comfort and satisfaction, to hope and quietly wait for the Lord’s salvation. Afflictions work very much for good: many have found it beneficial to bear this burden in their youth; it has made many humble and serious, and has weaned them from the world, who otherwise would have been proud and disobedient. If tribulation produces patience, that patience will produce experience, and that experience a hope that does not disappoint. A clear understanding of the evil of sin, and of our own sinfulness, will convince us that it is because of the Lord’s mercies we are not destroyed. If we cannot say with certainty, “The Lord is my portion,” may we not say, “I desire to have Him for my portion and salvation, and in His word do I hope?” We will be happy if we learn to receive affliction as laid upon us by the hand of God.

While there is life there is hope; and instead of complaining that things are bad, we should encourage ourselves with the hope they will be better. We are sinful people, and what we complain about is far less than our sins deserve. We should complain to God, not of Him. We tend, in times of calamity, to reflect on other people’s actions and blame them; but our duty is to examine and try our own ways, that we may turn from evil to God. Our hearts must go with our prayers. If inward feelings do not match outward expressions, we mock God and deceive ourselves.

The more the prophet looked at the desolations, the more he was grieved. Here is a word of comfort: While they continued weeping, they continued waiting; and they did not expect relief and help from anyone but the Lord.

Faith triumphs, for in these verses the prophet concludes with some comfort. Prayer is the breath of the new person, drawing in the air of mercy in requests, and returning it in praise; it proves and maintains spiritual life. He silenced their fears and quieted their spirits. You said, “Do not be afraid.” This was the language of God’s grace, by the witness of His Spirit with their spirits. And what are all our sorrows compared to those of the Redeemer? He will deliver His people from every trouble and revive His church from every persecution. He will save believers with everlasting salvation, while His enemies perish with everlasting destruction.”
